posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Amazing the intensity of flavor in such a lithe frame . . . .

This classic beauty actually gives the appearance, and has the taste, of a more advanced Bordeaux, almost something reminiscent of the 1970s. I would not have guessed 1996. Even the meniscus is starting to show just a hint of brown.

The bouquet is almost Graves, really lovely aromatics of dry earth, tobacco, red fruits and a soft perfume of old barn wood. Medium weight on the palate, perhaps a slight bit less, but packs a wallop of intensity that coats the tongue with a thin but lingering veneer of flavors. Wild red raspberry and red fruits, a little dip into the darks, but buttressed by some stemminess green, and closing with a really intriguing note of citrusy white soy sauce.

The tight tannins have a tea leaf quality with a little bitterness. They confuse somewhat, as it is the one thing that causes me to pause, wondering whether this wine really is at apogee. And as I think about this tannic finish, a very delicate but pronounced tannic finish, this wine could actually have some years to reach that pinnacle stage. I do not know this estate well enough to prognosticate, but if you have one, hold a few more years, if you have several, pop one and enjoy this classic wine.
(93 pts.)

posts.name: Corey Nøɤܝ©Ʞ
posts.cooked: Great note Roberto. Sounds like you caught it at a wonderful time.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: Saved 1/3 for tonight. A beautiful, distinct wine. I find solace here after seeing that list of Rollandized Chateaux!
Sounds great. Thanks for the note

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked: Great stuff Robert. I have to say I’m impressed that you knew of this, let alone bought it. Bel Air Marquis is a very well-kept secret even over here, let alone across the pond. It’s also very hard to find - I’ve no idea who he sells it to, since I’ve only seen it once in shops in the last 20 years. It occasionally comes up at auction but that’s about it. I can’t think of another geek wine like this in Bordeaux. I’m not actually surprised that you do like it, since it has a lot in common with Loire reds, but well done you.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
I think Julian just called me a nerd…

The Chinon reference is apropos, here is my note on the 2000:

WineBerserkers – 26 Jan 18

TN: 2000 Château Bel Air-Marquis d’Aligre, Margaux

Props to the new TWA hot-shot, William Kelley. He really nailed this wine, recommended it to me several months ago. It’s a gem. Decanted for two hours in perfectly cool, 60-degree, if not down-right nippy, Florida weather. Pretty tight red...

Reading time: 6 mins 🕑 Likes: 18 ❤

It was actually a young nerdy Brit that recommended it to me! Ha.

posts.cooked:
I had a corked 1970, my last bottle, last night. Such a pity.

Julian, Les Caves du Forum in Reims sells the wines.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
I think Julian just called me a nerd…

Too right I did! A badge of honour!

I think William you definitely qualify for the next Nerd Olympics too, which is surprising for a TWA scribe, although Neal Martin got a gold medal last time out for his musical taste.
Thanks for the tip - oddly enough, unlike with other regions, I don’t have the reflex of looking online for Bordeaux, so used I am to finding it on shelves. I don’t think Les Caves sell online, but I did find somewhere that does:

Vins Etonnants

Produits

Retrouvez les meilleurs vins d'auteurs, bio , biodynamiques et naturels. Expédition dans toute l'Europe et ouvert à l'international.

I shall get a few and report back.
They even have some 1970…at a price…!
